content.text: Accessibility Regression Gates in CI Pipelines

An accessibility regression gate blocks a pull request from merging when new WCAG violations are introduced. Without a gate, accessibility fixes decay silently: a developer adds a new chat feature, introduces an unlabelled button, and three sprints later a real screen reader user files a support ticket. The gate catches the regression at code-review time, before it ever reaches staging.

This sub-subtopic covers the three-layer gate used for GenAI chat applications — axe-core automated scan, Lighthouse CI score threshold, and pa11y diff against a stored baseline — along with baseline management, suppression rules, and the weekly expiry workflow.

attrs.alt: Three-layer accessibility CI gate pipeline with baseline management and suppression workflow

content.text: Layer 1 — axe-core Playwright Scan

The first layer runs axe-core inside Playwright against the built application on every pull request. It checks every route in the chat UI for WCAG 2.1 AA violations and fails the CI job if any are found.

# tests/a11y/test_axe_scan.py
import pytest
from playwright.sync_api import Page
from axe_playwright_python.sync_playwright import Axe

ROUTES = ["/", "/chat", "/settings", "/history"]
axe = Axe()


@pytest.mark.parametrize("route", ROUTES)
def test_no_wcag_violations(page: Page, route: str):
    """Fail PR if any WCAG 2.1 AA violations appear on this route."""
    page.goto(f"http://localhost:5173{route}")
    page.wait_for_load_state("networkidle")
    results = axe.run(page)
    violations = results.violations
    if violations:
        details = "\n".join(
            f"  [{v['impact'].upper()}] {v['id']}: {v['description']}\n"
            f"    Nodes: {[n['html'][:120] for n in v['nodes'][:3]]}"
            for v in violations
        )
        pytest.fail(f"{len(violations)} violation(s) on {route}:\n{details}")
Install with
pip install axe-playwright-python
. The
axe.run()

call injects axe-core into the page, runs all WCAG 2.1 AA rules, and returns a structured result. The failure message includes the violating HTML snippet so the developer can locate the element without running the app manually.
Configuring axe-core Rules
Not every axe rule is relevant to a GenAI chat UI. Disable rules that produce false positives for your specific tech stack and document the reason:
Keep the disabled-rule list short and reviewed. Each entry should have a comment explaining the exception.
Layer 2 — Lighthouse CI Score Gate

Lighthouse CI adds a higher-level accessibility score gate (0–1 scale). It checks a superset of issues beyond what axe-core covers, including missing document language, scroll area keyboard access, and heading order.

# .github/workflows/lighthouse.yml
name: Lighthouse CI

on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  lhci:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: "20" }
      - run: npm ci && npm run build
      - run: npx serve dist -p 3000 &
      - run: npx wait-on http://localhost:3000
      - name: Run Lighthouse CI
        uses: treosh/lighthouse-ci-action@v11
        with:
          urls: |
            http://localhost:3000/
            http://localhost:3000/chat
          configPath: .lighthouserc.json
          uploadArtifacts: true
Setting individual rules to
"error"
rather than relying solely on the aggregate score means a single missing button label fails the build regardless of the score average.
Layer 3 — pa11y Diff Gate

The axe-core and Lighthouse gates catch absolute violations. The pa11y diff gate catches regressions relative to the stored baseline — if main currently has 2 known issues and the PR introduces a third, the diff gate fails even if the Lighthouse score stays above 0.95.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/a11y-diff.sh
# Usage: ./scripts/a11y-diff.sh <base-branch>
set -euo pipefail

ROUTES=("/" "/chat" "/settings")
BASELINE=".a11y-baseline.json"
TMP=$(mktemp)

for route in "${ROUTES[@]}"; do
  npx pa11y "http://localhost:3000${route}"     --standard WCAG2AA     --reporter json >> "$TMP" 2>/dev/null || true
done

python3 scripts/compare_a11y.py "$BASELINE" "$TMP"
Updating the Baseline
The baseline file is committed to the repository. Update it deliberately when you intentionally fix or accept an issue:

# Re-generate baseline from current application state
npx pa11y "http://localhost:3000/" --standard WCAG2AA --reporter json   > .a11y-baseline.json

git add .a11y-baseline.json
git commit -m "a11y: update baseline after fixing label violations"
Never update the baseline in the same PR that introduces the regression. The baseline update should happen in a separate PR with a clear commit message.
Suppression Rules and Expiry

Some violations are known, tracked in a ticket, and scheduled for a future sprint. The suppression system allows them to pass the gate without blocking urgent work, while preserving a record and an expiry deadline.
The
expires_at
field is enforced by a weekly cron job. Any suppression past its expiry date causes CI to fail with a reminder:

# scripts/check_suppressions.py
import json
from datetime import date

with open(".a11y-suppressions.json") as f:
    data = json.load(f)

today = date.today()
expired = [
    s for s in data["suppressions"]
    if date.fromisoformat(s["expires_at"]) < today
]

if expired:
    for s in expired:
        print(f"EXPIRED suppression: {s['rule']} on {s['route']} "
              f"(ticket {s['ticket']}, expired {s['expires_at']})")
    raise SystemExit(1)

print(f"All {len(data['suppressions'])} suppressions are within their expiry dates.")
Rules That Cannot Be Suppressed
Some violations must block merge regardless of a suppression entry. These are written as a blocklist in the gate script:
PR Annotations and Reporting
CI violations should appear as inline PR annotations on the diff, not only in a log file that developers must scroll to find. Use the GitHub Actions
::error
command to annotate the file that introduced the violation:

# scripts/annotate_violations.py
import json, sys

with open(sys.argv[1]) as f:
    violations = json.load(f)

for v in violations:
    # Attempt to map violation to a source file via source map
    file_path = v.get("source_file", "src/components/Chat.tsx")
    line = v.get("source_line", 1)
    print(
        f"::error file={file_path},line={line},title=WCAG {v['impact'].upper()} - {v['id']}::"
        f"{v['description']} | Selector: {v.get('selector', 'unknown')}"
    )
When violations are annotated on the diff, reviewers see them without leaving the PR view. This removes the friction of cross-referencing CI logs with the code under review.
Monitoring and Trend Reporting

Beyond blocking individual PRs, track accessibility health as a metric over time. Record violation counts per route per week in a time-series store and display a trend chart in your engineering dashboard.
Metric
Tool
Alert threshold
New violations this week
pa11y diff gate
> 0 blocks PR
Lighthouse a11y score
Lighthouse CI
< 0.95 blocks PR
Expired suppressions
Weekly cron
> 0 files GitHub issue
axe critical violations
Playwright test
> 0 blocks PR
Manual test pass rate
Test spreadsheet
< 100% blocks release
Custom axe-core Rules for GenAI-Specific Patterns
The built-in axe-core rule set does not cover GenAI-specific patterns like an
aria-live="assertive"
on the message stream, or a thinking indicator that appears and disappears without announcing state. Write custom rules for these and register them before the scan:
Staging-Environment Accessibility Audit

The PR gate runs against a local build. A weekly audit runs against the staging environment with real data — AI responses that contain actual code blocks, tables, and multimodal content that the local mock server may not produce.

# .github/workflows/a11y-staging.yml
name: Accessibility Staging Audit

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 9 * * 1"   # Every Monday at 09:00 UTC

jobs:
  staging-audit:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: "20" }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx playwright install --with-deps chromium
      - name: Run audit against staging
        run: pytest tests/a11y/ -v --base-url=$STAGING_URL
        env:
          STAGING_URL: ${{ secrets.STAGING_URL }}
          # Auth token so the test user can log in and get real AI responses
          TEST_USER_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.A11Y_TEST_USER_TOKEN }}
      - name: Upload violation report
        if: failure()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: a11y-staging-report
          path: reports/a11y-violations.json
      - name: File GitHub issue on failure
        if: failure()
        run: |
          gh issue create             --title "Weekly staging a11y audit failed $(date +%Y-%m-%d)"             --body "See artifact: a11y-staging-report"             --label "a11y,automated"
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

The staging audit uses a real authenticated test user account so the LLM responds with actual output. This surfaces accessibility issues in content the model actually produces — such as a malformed table or a code block with an unusual language identifier — that a stub server would not generate.
Shared Accessibility Test Fixtures
The chat UI renders whatever the model returns. Test fixtures standardise the model responses used in accessibility tests so they cover the complete range of output formats:
Using fixtures rather than live LLM calls makes accessibility tests deterministic and fast. The same response is delivered every run so CI never fails because the model output a different format.
Summary

Accessibility regression gates prevent the gradual decay that occurs when each new GenAI feature introduces small violations that nobody notices until a screen reader user reports them. The three-layer gate — axe-core, Lighthouse CI, and pa11y diff — catches violations at different granularities.
axe-core Playwright scan catches specific WCAG rule violations with exact node selectors
Lighthouse CI score gate (≥ 0.95) catches document-level issues like missing language and heading order
pa11y diff gate catches regressions relative to the stored baseline so existing known issues do not mask new ones
Suppressions must include a linked ticket and an expiry date; an automated cron re-opens the gate when they expire
A short list of unsuppressible rules (missing label, keyboard trap, aria-live assertive) blocks merge unconditionally
PR annotations surface violations inline on the diff so developers fix them without context-switching to CI logs
